messages.content: shorten：α-SMA and NKG2D are used as markers for activating HSC and NK cells, respectively. Immunofluorescence double staining technique was used to observe the expression of activated HSC and activated NK cells in liver tissue. In short, paraffin slices were baked, dewaxed with xylene, and dehydrated with gradient alcohol. Sections immersed in 0.01M citrate buffer (pH 6.0) were placed in a microwave oven for antigen retrieval for 20min. Then 0.2% TritonX-100 was added for permeation, 3% H2O2 deionized water to inactivate endogenous peroxidase, and 10% goat/donkey serum (selected according to secondary antibody) was incubated at 37℃ for 2h to block non-specific antibodies, followed by incubation with mixture of anti-α-SMA (1:300, Abcam, United Kingdom) and anti-NKG2D (1:300, Abcam, United Kingdom) primary antibodies at 4℃ for overnight. The slices were washed with TBST for three times and then incubated with secondary antibody Alexa Fluor 488 (Abcam, United Kingdom) and Alexa Fluor 594(Invitrogen, United States) at 37℃for 1h followed by DAPI for another 15min. The slices were observed using a DMI8 Leica Inverted fluorescence microscope.

α-SMA and NKG2D are markers for activating HSC and NK cells, respectively. Immunofluorescence double staining was used to observe activated HSC and NK cells in liver tissue. Paraffin slices were prepared, dewaxed, and dehydrated. Antigen retrieval was performed using citrate buffer in a microwave oven. Permeation was achieved using TritonX-100, and endogenous peroxidase was inactivated with H2O2. Non-specific antibodies were blocked using goat/donkey serum, and a mixture of anti-α-SMA and anti-NKG2D primary antibodies was incubated overnight. After washing, secondary antibodies were applied, followed by DAPI. The slices were observed with a fluorescence microscope.

shorten：Liver tissues were fixed in 10% formalin and embedded with paraffin. After deparaffinization with xylene and rehydrate with graded ethanol, sections (4 μm) were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) using routine methods. Collagen analysis was performed by Sirius Red staining and quantified by Image-Pro Plus software (version 6.0; Media Cybernetics, Inc., Rockville, MD, USA). The liver tissue was distinguished from the background according to the difference in optical density to measure the entire liver tissue area, and then the percentage of positive fibrous tissue stained red was measured. Five microscopic fields were randomly observed in each section, and the average of the results measured in five different fields was taken as the semi quantitative value of each section.

Liver tissues were fixed in 10% formalin and embedded in paraffin. After deparaffinization with xylene and rehydration with graded ethanol, sections were stained with H&E using routine methods. Sirius Red staining was performed to analyze collagen, and quantification was done using Image-Pro Plus software. The liver tissue area was measured based on its optical density, and the percentage of positive fibrous tissue stained red was quantified. Five microscopic fields were randomly observed in each section, and the average of the results from these fields was used as the semi-quantitative value for each section.

shorten：Mice were randomly divided into normal group (group NORMAL, n = 5), liver fibrosis model group (group FIBROSIS, n = 10), and IL-10 gene intervention group (group IL-10, n = 10). Liver fibrosis was induced by 10 % CCl4 (5 ml/kg, intraperitoneal injection) diluted with olive oil, 2 times a week for 6 weeks. The mice in group NORMAL were injected with the same volume of olive oil as a control. From the third week, mice in group IL-10 were treated weekly with IL-10 gene intervention, and other mice were injected with the same dose of pcDNA3.1-Control as a control. By the end of the sixth week, all mice were sacrificed under anesthesia of 2% sodium pentobarbital. A brief modeling process is described in Figure 1(A).

Mice were divided into three groups: normal (n=5), liver fibrosis model (n=10), and IL-10 gene intervention (n=10). Liver fibrosis was induced by injecting 10% CCl4 (5 ml/kg, intraperitoneal) diluted with olive oil twice a week for 6 weeks. In the normal group, mice were injected with olive oil as a control. Starting from the third week, mice in the IL-10 group received IL-10 gene intervention weekly, while the other mice were injected with the control pcDNA3.1. At the end of the sixth week, all mice were sacrificed under 2% sodium pentobarbital anesthesia. The modeling process is illustrated in Figure 1(A).

shorten：Male 8-week-old specific pathogen-free (SPF)-grade C57BL/6J mice weighing 18 ± 2 g were purchased from Huafukang Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd(Beijing,China). The mice were raised at the Experimental Animal Center of Fujian Medical University in a controlled environment at 23–25°C, SPF-level environment, 40–60% humidity, and five mice per cage. Before the procedures, mice were adaptively reared in a 12-h light/dark cycle for 1 week, with ad libitum access to food and water. All procedures involving were conducted in accord with the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of the National Institutes of Health, and were approved by the Animal Welfare & Ethics Committee of the Fujian Medical University (no. FJMU IACUC2021-0273).

Male 8-week-old SPF-grade C57BL/6J mice (18 ± 2g) were obtained from Huafukang Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd(Beijing,China). Mice were housed at the Experimental Animal Center of Fujian Medical University under controlled conditions (23-25°C, SPF-level environment, 40-60% humidity) with five mice per cage. They were acclimated for 1 week in a 12-hour light/dark cycle with ad libitum access to food and water. All procedures followed the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of the National Institutes of Health and were approved by the Animal Welfare & Ethics Committee of Fujian Medical University (no. FJMU IACUC2021-0273).

shorten：pcDNA3.1-IL-10 plasmid and pcDNA3.1-control plasmid were purchased from Shanghai Genechem Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The preparation and injection of plasmid DNA were as described previously1. Briefly, mice were injected with 2.5ml of Ringer's solution (containing 100 μg pc-DNA3.1 Control or pcDNA3.1 IL-10) through a tail vein cannula without anesthesia.

pcDNA3.1-IL-10 and pcDNA3.1-control plasmids were obtained from Shanghai Genechem Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The preparation and injection of plasmid DNA followed previous protocols1. Mice were injected with 2.5ml of Ringer's solution containing 100 μg of either pcDNA3.1-Control or pcDNA3.1-IL-10 through a tail vein cannula without anesthesia.

shorten：Blood samples were obtained from mouse eyeball enucleation and allowed to
clot for 30min at room temperature. Serum was collected after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4℃. Serum levels of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) were measured by routine methods in the clinical laboratory of our institution.

Blood samples were collected through mouse eyeball enucleation and left to clot for 30 minutes at room temperature. Serum was obtained by centrifuging the samples at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes at 4°C. The levels of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) in the serum were measured using routine methods in our institution's clinical laboratory.

shorten：Liver tissues were collected and washed in cold Hank's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS). Immediately, the tissue was minced and passed through a 70 µm nylon mesh filter (BD Falcon, Millville, NJ, USA) to remove debris, and then the liver cell suspensions was centrifuged in cold PBS at 50 g for 5 min to eliminate hepatocytes. The supernatant containing the MNCs was collected and centrifuged at 1,600 rpm at 4℃ for 10min. The cell pellet was collected, resuspended in 40% Percoll (Sigma-Aldrich, St.Louis, MO, USA) in PBS, and then centrifuged at 2,400 rpm at 4℃ for 20 min without the brake. The supernatant was removed and red blood cells (RBC) were lysed by RBC lysis buffer（Solarbio, Beijing, China）for 2–5min, depending on the number of cells. The samples were then centrifuged at 1,800 rpm at 4℃ for 10min, resuspended in HBSS, counted, and prepared for flow cytometry.
100ul of fresh blood was added to 2ml of RBC lysis buffer. After lysis at room temperature for 5 min, centrifuged 450 g at 4℃ for 5 min to collect cells. Repeated the above process, resuspended cells in HBSS, counted, and prepared for flow cytometry.
Isolated MNCs were washed in PBS containing 1% fetal bovine serum. For detection of NK cells, cells were incubated at 37°C with 5% CO2 using BD Leukocyte Activation Cocktail (BD Biosciences) for stimulation. After, Fixable viability stain 510 and cd16/32 were added to the washed cells to distinguish dead cells and blocked nonspecific binding. Then, the cells were stained with fluorochrome-conjugated APC-Cy7-CD45, FITC-CD3, PE-NK1.1, PE-594-NKG2D, BV605-NKP46, PerCP-Cy5.5-CD11b, BV605-CD27, BV786-CD49a, APC-CD49b antibodies (BD Biosciences, San Jose, CA, USA). Intracellular cytokine staining was performed after permeabilization of cells using the BD Cytofix/Cytoperm kit (BD Biosciences) and stained with BV421-IFN-γ and R718-CD107a antibodies (BD Biosciences). Finally, after washed, the stained cells were detected on a BD FACS Celesta and analyzed using FlowJo V10 software (Tree Star, Ashland, OR).

Liver tissues were collected and washed with cold Hank's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS). The tissue was minced and passed through a 70 µm nylon mesh filter to remove debris. The resulting liver cell suspension was centrifuged in cold PBS to eliminate hepatocytes. The supernatant containing the mononuclear cells (MNCs) was collected and further centrifuged. The cell pellet was resuspended in 40% Percoll in PBS and centrifuged again. Red blood cells (RBCs) were lysed using RBC lysis buffer, and the samples were centrifuged, resuspended in HBSS, and counted for flow cytometry analysis.

For detection of NK cells, isolated MNCs were washed in PBS with 1% fetal bovine serum and incubated with BD Leukocyte Activation Cocktail for stimulation. Dead cells and nonspecific binding were distinguished using Fixable viability stain 510 and cd16/32. The cells were then stained with specific antibodies for surface markers using fluorochrome-conjugated antibodies. Intracellular cytokine staining was performed after permeabilization and staining with relevant antibodies. The stained cells were analyzed using flow cytometry.

In another procedure, fresh blood was treated with RBC lysis buffer, centrifuged, and the cells were collected. The process was repeated, and the cells were washed, counted, and prepared for flow cytometry analysis.

The flow cytometry analysis was performed using a BD FACS Celesta and analyzed using FlowJo V10 software.

shorten:To observe the importance of NK cells in the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10, study further established two experimental groups: NK cells deletion group (group FIBROSIS/ASGM1, n = 5), IL-10 gene intervention combined NK cells deletion group (group IL-10/ASGM1, n = 5). To deplete NK cells, mice were intraperitoneally injected with 20 μg of anti-ASGM1 (Wako, Richmond, VA) per mouse one day before IL-10 gene intervention2. To continuously deplete NK cells, mice were treated with anti-ASGM1 diluted in 0.1 mL of PBS twice a week. Mice in group FIBROSIS/IL-10 were injected with rabbit IgG as a control. The elimination of NK cells was confirmed by flow cytometry.

To study the role of NK cells in the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10, two experimental groups were established: the NK cells deletion group (FIBROSIS/ASGM1, n=5) and the IL-10 gene intervention combined with NK cells deletion group (IL-10/ASGM1, n=5). NK cells were depleted by intraperitoneally injecting 20μg of anti-ASGM1 per mouse one day before IL-10 gene intervention. Continuous depletion of NK cells was achieved by administering anti-ASGM1 twice a week in a volume of 0.1mL of PBS. The control group (FIBROSIS/IL-10) received injections of rabbit IgG. The elimination of NK cells was confirmed using flow cytometry.

SHORTEN:Blood samples were obtained from mouse eyeball enucleation and allowed to
clot for 30min at room temperature. Serum was collected after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4℃. Serum levels of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) were measured by routine methods in the clinical laboratory of our institution.

Blood samples were collected from mouse eyeball enucleation and allowed to clot for 30 minutes at room temperature. The serum was obtained by centrifuging the samples at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes at 4°C. Serum levels of AST and ALT were measured using routine methods in our institution's clinical laboratory.

SHORTEN:pcDNA3.1-IL-10 plasmid and pcDNA3.1-control plasmid were purchased from Shanghai Genechem Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The preparation and injection of plasmid DNA were as described previously1. Briefly, mice were injected with 2.5ml of Ringer's solution (containing 100 μg pc-DNA3.1 Control or pcDNA3.1 IL-10) through a tail vein cannula without anesthesia.

pcDNA3.1-IL-10 and pcDNA3.1-control plasmids were purchased from Shanghai Genechem Co., Ltd. The plasmid DNA preparation and injection followed previously described protocols1. Briefly, mice were injected with 2.5ml of Ringer's solution containing 100 μg of either pc-DNA3.1 Control or pcDNA3.1 IL-10 through a tail vein cannula without anesthesia.
